In Exodus 24, Israel encounters a fundamental paradox that defines the covenantal relationship with the living God: access granted through prescribed distance, intimacy purchased by blood, and promise sealed by an oath that demands obedience the people cannot keep. When God invites Moses and the elders to worship, He establishes clear boundaries. Only Moses approaches the throne; the others worship from a distance. This is not arbitrary rejection but the nature of holiness meeting sinfulness. Yet even more striking is what happens before they ascend the mountain. The people hear the covenant terms, declare unqualified commitment—twice—and Moses seals their pledge with blood ritual. Half the blood spatters the altar, representing God's acceptance. Half spatters the people, binding them to their own words. They have sworn an oath before witnesses and sealed it with sacrificial death. They have committed themselves completely. And in that moment of apparent triumph, they do not yet know how utterly they will fail to keep what they have promised.

What unfolds next reveals the grace that surrounds even Israel's covenant-making. Moses and the elders ascend and encounter something almost unimaginable: they see God Himself. Not a vision, not a representation, but the God of Israel—and they live. They see beneath His feet a work of sapphire pavement, a glimpse of divine majesty that would obliterate ordinary mortals. Yet God does not strike them down. Instead, the text tells us with almost casual wonder that they ate and drank. Communion with the living God becomes a meal. The Most High receives His covenant partners at His table. This meal is not a symbol of something more real; it is itself an encounter with the transcendent. In that moment, despite their imminent failure, despite the blood that binds them to commands they cannot obey, they experience a fellowship with God that anticipates something far greater than Sinai can hold.

The final ascent of Moses alone takes him into the cloud itself for forty days and nights—a period echoing the flood judgment, a number that will echo through Israel's wandering and Christ's resurrection. Moses enters the darkness where God speaks, and emerges with tablets of stone inscribed by God's own finger. But those forty days are crucial. While Moses receives the law in the cloud, the people below will create an idol. The very blood that sealed their covenant pledge will be spilled in judgment. The meal of peace will be followed by plague. The covenant that appeared so firm is already fracturing, not because God has failed in His promise, but because the people have already begun to break theirs. Yet God does not abandon the covenant. He will renew it, again and again, with Israel and finally with all believers through the blood of the One greater than Moses, the Mediator whose promise Israel's broken oaths could never keep, but whose blood alone can make sinners fit for the table of the Most High.
